Hey, Jordan, don't worry about the Wolf of Wall Street, time for the Wolf's Weekly Interactive Straight Line Sales Trading. Intensive and I swear to God, I'm going to find the shorter name for it this week because it's driving me crazy. You're probably driving me off the street. Do you want to have the right copy for this thing? Like, whether it's on a landing page or somewhere else, it drives everyone crazy. So I've got to figure out a short name. I'll open the suggestions, by the way. So sending your suggestions for a new name. And here's the thing also, is the program, you can just see it in the state, originally it started off, I said, all right, I'm going to do this one hour a week, an intense, so-called intensive trading for one particular aspect of the straight line, a deep dive, and unlike the bullshit free webinars of the world, there'll be a deep dive with all actionable strategies and no settling involved, right? So you don't have to worry about we wasting your time trying to sell you some shit at the end. I'm going to say something, I'll just say, hey, I got a product, that's it, right? We don't do that. This is a free, this is a network, everything we do in this network here is sales-free, while you're trading, right? But what happened was they originally said, you know, I'll do this weekly trading and I'll price it so incredibly low, I'll just make it this, just like, you know, a giveaway almost, and I picked this price of $9.95. And because that's really what Netflix does, I had Netflix in mind the whole thing, and I'm going to get millions of subscribers on this, that was my plan, right? And then I did it for a couple of weeks and it was working really, really well, right? So I start to formalize it and put a little more money into the production and hiring more staff. And then I said, you know, the secret at Stratton, really besides the straight line system, you know, before the straight line, there was literally this weird dichotomy where I was closing like a motherfucker, I was, you know, myself, I thought Danny to close rich people, but my guys couldn't do it. The 12 Strattonites could not close one rich person. They were able to close poor people. We were calling average moms and pops when we first started. We switched to calling rich people. They couldn't do it. I was doing it. The firm almost shut down because of that. And I had a necessity, had to come up with a new way for training sales people. So it was so effective, so easy to learn, and basically idiot proof, because for the most part, they were idiots. I know you're not an idiot, but, you know, these kids, the average IQ was, you know, far as scum on three hits of acid, basically. And even more important than that, this might very well be the case, because they weren't natural born salespeople. That's the more important aspect. So, you know, sales IQ is not so about about, you know, intellectual IQ, it's about, are you a natural born closer or not? Do you have the ability to essentially trigger the right tonality, apply it to the words that you say automatically, is your brain organized in a way that you know how to make these airtight logical cases? Is it intuitive for you or not? Some people are just really, you know, they're brilliant. They have great ideas. They have amazing discoveries they've made. But because they can't verbalize and they can express themselves in a way that connects with other human beings and makes them want to take action, either to buy it or simply help them make their idea into reality, they suffer. They live a life that is far less robust, far less empowered, and also far less wealthy, meaning the material stuff, then they easily could have lived if they had been proficient in the art of communication. Because that's really what the straight line is. It's a communication strategy, you know, an intensely powerful one at that, it allows you to communicate any thought, idea, hope, dream, casual, intangible, offline, on anything that you want to communicate that to another person in a way that connects with them, makes them want to be a part of it or simply take action and buy what you're selling and it gets you what you want to, which is your outcome, your vision, the process, several business. That's what the straight line is, right? So when I started Stratton, I had that pickup where they couldn't close, I had to invent a new system and that became the straight line. However, the secret to the success of the firm was also in the fact that after I invented the straight line, every single morning, I would stand up in front of the firm, you know, as it grew, it started with 12, it was pretty soon it was 1200, right? You know, and then, you know, it got to this high, it's like 3000 or 4000, it was crazy, right? And I stood in front of the firm and I would give this 15 to 25 minute and it wasn't just a pump up session, it was a combination of motivation, real motivation at the highest level and skills training. So I would keep them sharp on their skills and motivate them each day. And because of that intervention, that daily intervention, it became literally almost a gallery, pretty much a gallery, 100% of the people who walked in succeeded. That's how that was the truth. No one failed at becoming an expert closer. And I, you know, do like quick, you know, 15 minute pump up and I sort of really enjoyed it myself because these things are coming out awesome. So the point is, is that the reason I had them get into this elevated state was because the ability to manage your state, your physiological emotional state, and that is the topic of today. How do you manage your emotional state in a way that makes you a ton more money? Notice, I'm going to take this now. Let's make it operational. Let's bring it in so you can use this in your day to day life as you sell, as you negotiate, as you make decisions to make sure that you're in the highest level state for whatever state you need to be in. In other words, there are certain states in life that lead people to massive success and wealth. And by the way, in sales, those states specifically, let's put a slide up here if we can. Okay, the states are number one, I called them, I'm writing the board also, so you call them the four C's. Okay, the four C's. And you've probably heard me say this, but today I'm going to really dig in deeper and I'm going to give you actionable strategies here, especially one big one that allows you to ensure that you're in a peak state every time you enter a sales encounter, whether it's picking up the phone, walking into someone's door, someone walks in your door, I'm going to show you how to get into a peak state. So we call these the four C's. Those states are certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage, the four C's. And essentially what this means is that when you're in one of these states, it opens you up, it allows you to access the skills inside you, the resources inside you, the talent that you have, the natural talent, right? When you are in this state, one of these states, it allows you to be the best that you can be. In professional sports, we call being in the groove and flow, right? When you see, you know, guys really in the groove right there, he's just, wow, it's guys to smash it. Like when you see Federa, they call it in God mode and he's doing things that you like, they would see even console with a racket. We see it in all sports, it's like this extreme state of focus and clarity and just where they're able to access every last bit of the skill they have in that moment. So I want you to start thinking of state management. That's the phrase we use, state management, the ability to manage your, essentially to intervene on your current state, the way you feel as you see fit. To artificially put yourself in this, artificially put yourself in a state that serves you. In other words, in sales, right? I've said this many times to you, sales is the transference of emotion. That's what you're doing at the highest level. Sales is the transference of emotion. And the primary emotion that you're transferring is certainty. There's certainty about yourself, certainty about the product you're selling, certainty about the company that you work for, they'll stand behind the product. There's also, as I said in a daily motivation last week, as if this other like sort of baseline certainty, that everything that you say, no matter what tonality you're using, there's always certainty behind it. Like that you're selling the line, sort of thing. You're selling the scene, as I say, in the acting world. And I don't mean that in a disingenuous way. I mean that actually in a genuine way. That you should be authentic with what you're saying, but that it comes across to people believe you. So that when you're talking with sincerity or empathy, that people actually, they're certain that you really are empathetic. So in sales, you always have this sort of baseline certainty that carries through no matter what you're saying that what tonality you're using is always certainty that yes, he seems authentic. She seems authentic. What they're saying is accurate. It makes sense. It's congruent and so forth. Right. So on one side, you have these four states, certainty to be absolutely certain about the product you're selling, that you're the right salesman for them, that you're a great salesman who will stay behind the product, that you do great customer service, that you'll be ethical, honest, right? You'll be fit at the long term, certainly by the company, right? That's your basic, that's what's basic certainty, right? And then on the other side of the equation, you have what's called uncertainty. So imagine if you walk into a sale, but you're feeling uncertain, and it doesn't, you essentially can't transfer certainty that you don't have. So the straight line system, it allows you to transfer the certainty that you have in size. You're feeling certain, right? You just know you're the best, you know, you're prox, the best, your company, right? And you're sure of it, you're absolutely certain. And the straight line allows you to get that point across to someone else. You get it? But what the straight line doesn't do, it doesn't actually create certainty. You do that, you create certainty. The straight line transfers it. The same way I used this metaphor before, where you have in the basement of a house, you might have the furnace, the boiler, right? It creates heat, creates the heat. Then you have the HVAC, the ductwork and so forth, that transfers the heat around the house. So you might have all these great beautiful ducts, they work really well, they're clean, they're brand new. But if the boiler ain't working, there's nothing to transfer. So that's why it's so important that you are feeling absolutely certain inside. You get it? That oozing at you, essentially. And then the straight line system allows you to transfer that to anybody that you're communicating with, your prospect, could be in a negotiation, so when you're looking to hire, it doesn't matter what the situation is. Any situation of influence, the straight line allows you to transfer the certainty. But again, if you went in that moment, imagine walking through someone's door and you're feeling uncertain about yourself, uncertain about your product, you have nothing to transfer. So then you can't move them up on the certainty scale to that magical 10, 10, 10, so you're basically screwed. Same thing goes for clarity. The opposite of clarity is overwhelm. So when you're essentially feeling clear about something, it all makes sense. Seems easy, you know where you're going, right? Got a clear picture, overwhelming, oh my god, I, you know, just so much, I can't sort through the mess. What do I do first? What do I do second? And that's why when people are in a state of overwhelm, what do they do? Nothing, they shut down. Overwhelm is one of the most destructive states for salespeople and entrepreneurs. Because when you're in that state, you don't do anything. And anything you do, you don't do very well. Same thing goes for confidence, right? So, you know, you're feeling confident about something, you're just totally in it, right? You get it, you know, confidence is like, you know, I just know that I'm going to get what I want. I have this great outcome, I'm going to get it, I'm going to close the deal, right? Versus being riddled with self-doubt. If you're riddled with self-doubt, it oozes through you. You understand, like, I don't want to belabor this because this is the very basic stuff here, but I need to lay the foundation for the strategy I want to teach you. So it's important that you understand that it's sort of like there's always two sides to these coins with states. You have certainty, then you have uncertainty, you have clarity, then you have overwhelm, you have courage, you have fear, right? You have confidence, you have self-doubt. You get it? Two sides to every coin. So the point is, imagine walking through someone's door to make a sale and when you pick up the phone and try to do calls when you're in a state of uncertainty and you're feeling fearful and you lack confidence, okay, and you're scared, you're feeling, you know, mad, you're overwhelmed, good luck trying to close the sale. You get it? You imagine this, it's like almost like there's a valve inside you, like a spigot, right? When you're in an empowered state of certainty, clarity, confidence, it opens up the spigot and allows the best of you, all the training, all the natural ability, all the skills you have, it allows us to flow out effortlessly. When you're in a disempowered state, the spigot's closed off, like a water spigot and nothing can flow. So yeah, the water's still inside there and all your talent is in there, but it can't flow, it can't leave your body and go out into the world and make an impact. It can't essentially elevate other people to a state. And if you're in an uncertainty, you're fucking screwed, you're done, you get it? So that's the key to that little valve. Now remember, as in most things in life, this shade's a gray. It's definitely that valve's completely closed, right? So you're not going to keep it loose, unless you're really screwed up. You don't walk around all day long riddled with uncertainty and riddled with self-doubt and in a state of absolute fear. You know those people are shuddings, right? You don't leave their fucking houses. There's probably not that many of you, probably none of you, on this training, right? But what there are shades of this where you have this ultra-high level of certainty, clarity, confidence, courage, right? That allows you to be in flow, to be in the groove when you are, like, having one of those days where everything that you say, everything that you do, every word that escapes you, you're like, damn, I'm fucking good, right? You're like, just, you're like, holy shit, I'm impressing myself today, right? And then you have those other days where, you know, you do things and you gotta put your foot in your mouth, you don't kind of hit the tennis ball as good as you can. Maybe your rebuttals are as tight, your sales pitch isn't as clear, right? Your smile's not as bright, and it's shades of gray. So you're not going to ever have typically this total negative, but far and few between very, very rare is someone that has the ability that before they walk through the door, before they pick up the phone, before they enter any important situation, then they can instantly snap into those states, these ultra-empowered states at the highest level, no matter what's going on in their life. In other words, it's easy to feel good and to be certain when the outside world is giving you all the cues that trigger those feelings organically. In other words, if everything's going great, you're closing every single person that day, yeah, it's pretty easy to pop into that peak state, right? But that's fantasy land. That's not the way things work in the real world. You know, we have good days, in terms of empirically, the outside forces, there's good things, there's bad things, you have problems at home, you have great days at home. So the point is, is that you're going to walk, you know, if you let's look at this over a period of a month, you're going to walk into, we're going to work each day and some days you're naturally, based because of environmental factors and just how you feel inside, you're going to walk in, let's draw a graph here, right? You're going to walk in and here's one, here's 10. 10 is like a combination of those to the best, here's the worst of those states, right? Every once in a while, you're going to walk in and you're going to just naturally, because of what's going on in your life and how you feel, how you slept and bam, you're in that 10, you're just feeling absolutely certain and you're damn sure of it, right? That's going to happen some days and then probably once a year maybe, you know, you're just going to be like almost like a poop screw, like when you just feel shitty, you can't, you know, then you'll be even sick by the way, when you're just like the worst uncertain, whatever's going on, your wife just left you, the landlords are victing you, the last 50 people fucking slam the door in your face, everyone hung up the phone on you haven't closed in a week, whatever's going on, your boss told you you're an asshole, you go over and tell you you're a bigger asshole, all right? And you're as close to one, right? Well now imagine your closing rate, imagine so you have a baseline, here's your 100%, so on your best day, in other words, based on your natural talent and how much training you have with the straight line, you're going to have a baseline closing rate, where you'll close let's say, five out of 10 people, on your best day, you don't close everyone, this is not the way the world works, right? So on your best day, you'll close five, that's when you're at a 10, here, as you slide down here and your state diminishes each day, at any given day, this will go to four, three, two point, you get it? So you can access the resources that you have, okay? for sales here, this applies to all aspects of life, right, so okay, if you're a salesman, right, let me say it slowly here, watch, you're a salesperson, and you knock on someone's door, and in that moment, you're in a state of fear, uncertainty, self doubt, you're just not feeling right, I don't care how great of a salesman that you are, in that moment, how good of a salesman because you can't access your greatness, you're blocked, you're blocked, same thing goes for a parent, let's say, you know, you have kids, right, many you probably do some though, doesn't matter, imagine you're a parent, you have a couple of kids, you'll love them to death, you probably read some books on parenting at this point, right, so you know some of the skills of parenting, right, and you typically are a great parent, but let's say you come home from work, you walk in the door, and you're in a state of overwhelm and anger and impatience, well how good of a parent can you be in that moment, not very much, not very good parent, you're blocked from accessing your parenting skills, in the same way a salesperson is blocked from accessing their sales skills, that's why state management is so important, so crucial, and that's why every morning I made my meeting with a rah-rah, getting people into an elevated state, now just so you know, I didn't call it state management, I never heard of state management, we called it getting negative back then, you gotta be positive, getting pumped up, so intuitively I knew that is before I started studying this stuff, really, and made it into a system, I knew that if my guys were negative, that they just couldn't close at the same level, so even during the day, at any point during the day, I saw the energy dip, and everyone just like losing that pump, that certain thing, I'd stop, I'd make everyone put down the phone, I'd do a quick two-inch for me to pump up, get them back on the phone, watching the sales, we'd go right back up to the highest level, that's how important state management is, didn't call it back then, but that's what it is, okay, so now I want to explain to you exactly, it's specific now, so you get it, everyone, now that I've explained how important, how crucial this is, let me show you a sure-fire way, a system I've created, for triggering a peak state of certainty at will, now remember, what this is not, this is not about being in a state of absolute certainty all the time, you don't want to walk around in a state of certainty I mean constantly, I mean what do you call someone who walks around all the way along with their chest puffed out, and then honestly, their shoulders pulled back, and their head held high, and having a little bit, what do you call someone like that, we call them an asshole, right, so that's not what I'm saying, I'm talking about state management in the sense that in those crucial moments, when it matters most, triggering a peak state right beforehand, which means essentially blocking out the outside forces that have caused you to land at a certain state organically, so organically in that moment when you're about to go into a situation of influence, you might be at a six organically, I'm going to show you how to intervene on that, and bring yourself up to an absolute ten, and damn sure of it, that's a val-, that's an invaluable skill, and I'm telling you, once you learn this, I've taught this around the world and it's so liberating, and now I'm going to teach you at a very deep level, really deep enough, I've taught it before because I'm really digging in here, and I want you to understand how important it is, this is why I spent so much time building up to this moment, so let's get specific now, my journey with state management started, I mean I started calling it state management, I started studying what's called NLP, you guys probably have heard of NLP, okay, NeuroLinguistic Programming, and I had the benefit of studying directly who taught Richard Bama who invented NLP, a little guy named Brinder, okay, but Bama is really the famous one, he was like, you know, the personality behind it, and I think he was more of the psychologist, Brindan might have been more of the numbers guy, but maybe I'm wrong, so don't quote me on that, alright, so NLP stands for NeuroLinguistic Programming, alright, and what this is about, it's a system of advanced psychology that's about making near instant changes in your personality by treating your brain almost like it's a computer, and that essentially because your brain functions like a computer, that if you understand what the language is, what software, right, you know, essentially what language your brain speaks, which is actually English, whatever language you speak, that's the software of the brain, and if you know how to write code, you can intervene and change your patterns, you can actually intervene in yourself and change your patterns, right, there's a lot of different applications for NLP, and you know, and many of them are really powerful, some are as powerful, there are two that I believe are extremely powerful, one of them is called a timeline regression, and I actually did that with you guys once, I did a timeline regression that I learned from Bama, alright, where you go in and you use this to change belief systems, so you actually intervene on your beliefs by going to like not a hypnotic state, but going to an intense state of concentration, knowing back and actually, you know, essentially altering the way you look at things and reordering your beliefs, very powerful stuff, that's the first one, the second thing is this process called angering, that's the second very powerful process I found in NLP, the problem was is that it didn't really work for me, in other words, I, I don't want to explain, let me explain what anger is, but I want you to see that, I think it's great what they did with NLP, but the problem was, is as brilliant as it was, it wasn't actually that usable for most people that I tried to teach you to and myself in the real world, in business, in sales, as an, you just, you couldn't essentially use the strategy, but what angering was about, was about essentially setting some sort of outside trigger, so imagine you're the story of Pavlov's dog, let me just take a sip of my uh, red bull hill, actually I'll tell you it's Rockstar, okay, you know the story of Pavlov's dogs, right, so Russian scientists back in the late 1800s, Ivan Pavlov, he had this really loud bell, a starving dog and a juicy piece of meat, right, so what he did was testing out, you know, how that, what, about influencing patterns and what he did is he had this starving dog and he would ring this giant, this really loud bell at the same moment he would introduce to the dog's vision a piece of juicy filet mignon, right, and what happened is, is that when the dog saw the filet mignon, his mouth would start to water and the bell was ringing in the background, which was coincidental and then he did it the next day again and he had a starving dog, same dog, right, and he introduced the piece of meat in the same instant he rang this really loud bell, well pretty soon what he found out was that he didn't even have to introduce the meat anymore, the mere sound of the bell was enough to make the dog salivate, because ever in the beginning he'd shown the dog the meat, it's salivate, right, after five or ten times what happened, the dog would simply salivate by hearing the bell, so the dog essentially had linked the sound of the bell to a piece of filet mignon and salvation and after that link became strong enough over time you could essentially remove the meat, ring the bell and the dog would salivate, makes sense, right, you probably all heard of that experience, Pavlov's dog, right, so essentially what happened was a Pavlov hypothesized which was correct and through these linkages forming in the brain that you know, so this sort of three things, you know, dog starving sees meat causes salvation naturally, that's a natural response, but the ringing of the bell happens at the same time, the brain eventually links the two together so strongly after a while, especially if it's really intense, that's why it's a starving dog, a beautiful piece of meat and a loud bell, you know, a bell that's with the dead basically, right, so essentially what happens is the dog links up the bell with salvation, in that experiment the bell is called your, that ringing bell is called your anchor, it's an, you set an anchor and it's called firing off your anchor when you rig it, so essentially in that situation the bell, the loud bell that the wake the dead is an anchor for the dog and when you ring the bell the terminology we use is called firing off an anchor, you get it, so you fire off an anchor and in this case the dog would salivate, well many years later Dr. Baylor had this theory that you know human beings aren't that much different than dogs and he worked on this strategy in part of NLP, State Imagine called an anchoring and developed a very very powerful strategy and theory for getting someone to essentially get anchor into a certain state, so imagine if it was that simple, you could ring the bell and imagine if you had linked it up to a state of certainty and every time you rang the bell, when you went through a state of absolute certainty, right, if you've ever attended a Tony Robbins event, right, one of the UPW, right, no one does it better than him, he's an expert in that moment of getting people into a state of absolute certainty, with the music is playing, the lights are flashing, the dancers are on stage, he's screaming, he's jumping, he's eight foot nine, he's fucking heads of the size of fucking catch, it's at everyone's freaking out, and everyone's like yes, yes, yes, everyone's like yes, yes, now like oh my god I set an anchor, unfortunately this doesn't work, seriously, very, I mean like 0.01% of the people are actually set an anchor, in other words they feel great in that moment, yes, he got them to a very high level of certainty and feeling great, but doesn't work like that in the real world, very very hard to set an anchor, because what's happening is this, and here's the operative thing, okay, there is essentially as Barry and Barry explained the whole thing to me, because he recognizes the challenges of anchor, right, because it's such a powerful process that if it works, if you really set an anchor, fuck, it's like unbelievable, you know it's a great example of the anchor, get a bit in your car, and you're driving down the road, you're not speeding, right, but all of a sudden you see the flashing lights in here, in a microsecond, you're hot speeding fast, right, your pulse is racing, you're at height, you know, look, before you even know it, before you have any chance to see if it's for you, someone else, your body instantly, your autonomic nerves instantly reacts and puts you into a state of absolute awareness and worry, right, and fear, right, that's an example of an anchor, it's only five, three seconds later maybe when you see the, oh, it's on the other side of the road, well, the guy's compass passing you by, that your pulse starts to calm down again, your breathing relaxes, that's an anchor, so why isn't an anchor like that get set naturally and so hard to set an anchor for a certain deal of compass, why is that, well, here's the answer, that anchors only get set when you're at the, this is the million dollar answer to the whole thing, when you're at the tippy top of the state and then at that moment when you're really at the tippy top of the state, something weird, something that you can't ignore, like a flashing light, a siren or a loud bell in the case of paddles, something that's introduced naturally that causes you to associate the two, see here's the deal, in reality it only takes one time to set an anchor, you can set an anchor in one shot, but you would have to be at the absolute tippy top of the state, so imagine one to a hundred, right, and a hundred means like this is really, this is like So here's how NLP anchoring works. NLP anchoring, very quickly, is based on the following steps. Number one, choose a state that you want to anchor, right? Step two, go back and search a memory, go for a root memory, where you actually in that thing, go back to a moment in time when you were in that state. Step three, use the power of the brain to essentially manipulate this memory to intensify it. And I'll explain it in a second, so that's make the memory more clear, make it brighter, make it louder, I'll officially do that, right? And then the next step is to link it to a sound or something or a nail sticking in the word, yes, a word, a sound, something, right? Link it to an anchor and five, okay? Repeat the process again and again until it sets. That's NLP in a nutshell. Now, the way, the only thing that goes away is the stepping of the brain. What they do, it's as bandless brilliant, by the way, was he found out that the brain is actually programmable and you can make very quick changes in yourself, which is why it's a popular and self development. And the way this works is just follows, right? We have five cents. How the five cents is how you make sense of the word, right? You have, for most people, the dominance visual, then you have auditory, then you have kinesthetic, you have olfactory, and you have gustatory. Visual is what you see, auditory what you hear, kinesthetic what you feel, olfactory what you smell, and gustatory what you taste. Those are your five senses, your five sensory modalities, right? But it gets more complicated because you have visual external what you see in the world, or you can close your eyes and you have visual internal what you can remember from the past. You get it? Taking it one step further, you have visual internal recall, you can see what you experienced in the past, or visual construction. You can actually invent something, you can take any memory you have, and you can change it, manipulate it, make it more intense, okay? Same thing goes for auditory, by the way. You have your external auditory, what you hear come sounds out of the world, also you close your eyes, if you want to close your eyes, and you can remember sounds that you've heard, or you can actually create sounds. Imagine that you hear something, you hear voices, or create dialogue, right? Same thing goes for kinesthetic, kinesthetic what you feel, like this what you feel, or a gut feeling that you have, you have gut feelings, this kinesthetic, right? And then you have what you feel from the outside, or when you close your eyes, you imagine feeling, right? And again, it always has construction, you can make it up, or it's actual recall. Same thing goes for olfactory, and custom olfactory smells, you have olfactory, and by the way, of all these senses, you know what the most powerful one is, viscerally, sense of smell. This is the one by the way. Now, human beings are visual creatures, most of them, mostly visual and auditory, so we all have dominant senses, but most of us is visual and auditory, and some of us are also kinesthetic, what you see, what you hear, and what you feel, right? Olfactory is not really, if you're a lab of overtreatment, yes, if you're most important sense probably, but for human beings, it's more of a secret, live without it, although it enriches your life. And gustatory is what you taste, very important for a chef, for a wine taster, but probably not as important for someone day to day, you can get by without having a great sense of taste. Although imagine though, when you were out in, you know, maybe 20,000 years ago, when you were tasting foods in the wild, much more important, that's what was poisonous, what was, right, you get it? So that stuff, not anymore. Now it doesn't play as much, but remember this, by far the most powerful sense of all, it sets a smell. It goes directly to the lumbic system, in the base of the brain, that reptilian brain, it's like when you smell one molecule of something, you probably experienced this many times, you're like, oh my God, I'm back in my grandmother's house because of some smell of food, of a sofa, you know, or like if you're in a freshly cut field of grass for me, one molecule of that, I'm back in day camp when I was like, you know, eight years old, six years old, I'll never forget the smell of freshly cut grass every time I even get one with it, I'm right back there. My biggest anchor of all was, I had this bad experience when I was like five years old, my parents took me to the circus, right, peanuts and elephant shit, the smell of peanuts and I vomited, right, to this day, if I don't have the service, I have to fight back the fucking vomit, that one little molecule of smell and damn, I'm back.
